title: What I think about Prophets of Doom, Munster by Dan Carlin Podcast - Requested by C55365

enrichment.summary: Kyle reviews Dan Carlin's 'Prophets of Doom' podcast episode about the Anabaptist takeover of Münster in 1534–1536, retelling the story of Jan van Leiden, the siege, famine, and brutal executions. He connects the Münster rebellion to broader themes of the Reformation, class disruption, millennialism, iconoclasm, and draws parallels to modern politics including Trump, Christian nationalism, and economic inequality.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Very effective, immersive storytelling; worth listening to twice
Driven by class disruption, millennialist fervor, Reformation upheaval, and possibly ergot poisoning
Follows a predictable arc: prophet → monopoly on God's word → persecution of dissenters → sexual control → collective destruction
A recurring pendulum between wealth-celebration and wealth-rejection; destroying cultural heritage is a loss
Trump is breaking laws (Emoluments Clause, insurrection bar) and parallels authoritarian millennialist movements; a revolution of the poor may be coming
Likely tied to 2033 being 2,000 years after Jesus's death, repeating the millennial pattern seen in 1000 and 1533
Early Christian principles of sharing and caring for the poor were genuinely proto-communist and gave rise to hospitals
First reformer to survive challenging the Church; later used by Nazis; likely was constrained by his political protector

entities: Jan Bockelson (Jan Hus — Kyle conflates names)
Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire (Gibbon)
